exploration_log.aha_moment: The existing code uses `names[i]` (positional) to match the i-th constructor parameter to the i-th column. The fix is a nested loop: for each constructor parameter, search all column names for a match, then use the found column index for the type check.
exploration_log.hindsight: Should also consider the case where `names.Length != types.Length` could be an issue, but the existing code already ensures `ctorParameters.Length == types.Length` so this is safe.

alternatives: Build a dictionary of name->index for O(1) lookup instead of O(n^2) nested loop
key_decisions.decision: Use a nested loop to find the column index by name for each constructor parameter
key_decisions.reasoning: Correct and simple; constructor counts are small so O(n^2) is acceptable. A dictionary would be more efficient for large column counts but adds complexity.

solve_notes: Changed `FindConstructor` from positional matching (`names[i]` for the i-th parameter) to name-based matching: for each constructor parameter, a nested loop searches all column names for a case-insensitive match, then uses the found column index for type compatibility checks.
task_text:
Fix DefaultTypeMap.FindConstructor using positional rather than name-based matching

`DefaultTypeMap.FindConstructor` in `DefaultTypeMap.cs` matches
constructor parameters to query columns by position (array index) rather
than name. When query columns appear in a different order than the
constructor parameters, the match fails or binds the wrong values. Fix
`DefaultTypeMap.cs` so `FindConstructor` matches each constructor
parameter to its corresponding column by name (case-insensitive),
regardless of column order.

thrash_preventing_defs.name: GetConstructorMap
thrash_preventing_defs.reason: read: an AI agent would check how constructor parameter-to-column index mapping is used downstream to ensure its colIndex approach matches expectations

tier_difference_reasoning: The minimum set is just `FindConstructor` since that is the only edited method. `GetConstructorMap` (downstream consumer) is added to thrash_preventing because an AI agent would want to verify that the colIndex it computes is used correctly downstream in the mapping pipeline. Seeing it prevents an agent from guessing about whether the index semantics match what the caller expects.
